heroOverview: Erythrasma is a superficial bacterial infection of the skin caused by Corynebacterium minutissimum, a Gram-positive rod that colonises the outermost layer of the epidermis (stratum corneum) in warm, moist, occluded body folds. Unlike superficial fungal infections that it closely mimics, erythrasma produces no hyphae; instead, the bacterium generates porphyrins that cause a distinctive coral-red fluorescence under Wood's ultraviolet lamp, which is the most rapid and specific diagnostic sign. The condition presents as well-demarcated, reddish-brown, slightly scaly, flat patches in the groin, armpits, toe webs, and inframammary folds. Prevalence estimates vary widely but generally range from 1-8% of adults in temperate climates, rising to 20-25% in tropical and subtropical regions. Erythrasma is non-contagious in most cases, responds readily to topical or oral antibiotics, and almost never causes systemic illness, though it tends to recur when predisposing conditions — obesity, diabetes, sweating, and immunosuppression — persist.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 4-8% prevalence in temperate climates; up to 25% in tropical and humid regions (studies in dermatology literature)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adults aged 20-60; higher in those with obesity, diabetes, or who live in hot humid climates
keyStats.avgAge: Peak presentation in middle-aged adults, though all ages are affected
keyStats.globalCases: A common but frequently misdiagnosed superficial skin infection; exact global burden is uncertain due to underreporting and misattribution to tinea cruris

definition: Erythrasma (ICD-10: L08.1) is a chronic, superficial bacterial infection of the skin confined to the stratum corneum, caused by Corynebacterium minutissimum — a small, non-motile, Gram-positive diphtheroid rod that is part of the normal skin flora in many individuals. When conditions of warmth, moisture, and occlusion allow it to multiply beyond its commensal threshold, it colonises the superficial keratin layer and produces porphyrins as metabolic byproducts. These porphyrins are responsible for the diagnostic coral-red or pink fluorescence seen under a Wood's lamp (365 nm ultraviolet light) and for the brownish-red discolouration of the lesions in natural light. The condition falls under dermatology and is classified as a superficial bacterial dermatosis. Clinically, it presents as sharply marginated, brown-red, mildly scaly, flat plaques without vesicles or pustules — features that distinguish it from pyoderma and from the superficial dermatophyte infection tinea cruris, which it most commonly mimics. Erythrasma is benign and non-contagious under ordinary circumstances; it does not invade below the stratum corneum in immunocompetent individuals. However, in patients with diabetes, immunosuppression, or HIV infection, more extensive or recalcitrant forms can occur, and the toe-web form can act as a portal for lower limb bacterial cellulitis.

typesClassification.description: The most common form; affects skin folds — the groin and inner thighs, axillae, and inframammary area — where moisture, warmth, and friction allow C. minutissimum to flourish; presents as reddish-brown, flat, scaling plaques.

Affects the web spaces between toes, particularly the 4th and 5th toe clefts; produces whitish maceration, fissuring, and scaling that closely resembles tinea pedis; this form can breach the skin barrier and predispose to lower limb cellulitis.

Less common variant presenting as isolated coin-shaped brownish plaques on the trunk or extremities, often in patients with diabetes or immunosuppression; lesions are flat, non-elevated, and may be extensive.

primarySymptoms: Reddish-brown or tan, flat patches in body folds — the hallmark presentation, typically in the groin, inner thighs, armpits, or under the breasts — with well-defined, irregular borders.
Fine, bran-like scale on the surface of the plaques, lighter than the scaling of tinea infections and without the papular edge of ringworm.
Mild to moderate itching (pruritus) in the affected skin fold, which is often the main complaint prompting medical attention.
Skin maceration or softening in the toe-web form, where the spaces between toes become white, moist, and friable.
Fissuring in toe-web erythrasma, where macerated skin splits to produce painful linear cracks that can bleed and serve as a portal for bacterial infection.
A slight wrinkling or crepe-paper texture of the affected skin, reflecting superficial stratum corneum damage without deeper dermal involvement.
Coral-red or pink fluorescence under a Wood's lamp — not visible to the naked eye but one of the most specific diagnostic signs in dermatology, produced by the porphyrins of C. minutissimum.
Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic presentation in a proportion of affected individuals, particularly in truncal or inframammary lesions that are not subjected to friction.
Darkening or hyperpigmentation of older lesions compared with surrounding skin, which may persist for weeks after successful treatment as post-inflammatory pigment change.
Absence of vesicles, pustules, or pus — unlike pyoderma, erythrasma does not generate a purulent exudate, which helps distinguish the two conditions clinically.
earlyWarningSigns: A faintly reddish-brown patch appearing in a skin fold that feels slightly rough to the touch and does not respond to antifungal cream within two to three weeks.
Subtle itching in the groin or inner thigh without any visible fungal scaling or ring-shaped border.
Softening or whiteness between the fourth and fifth toe web spaces that does not clear with foot hygiene.
A persistent brownish discolouration in the armpit or inframammary fold in a person with diabetes or obesity.
emergencySigns: Rapidly spreading redness, swelling, and pain in a limb, particularly the lower leg, in a person with toe-web erythrasma — may indicate cellulitis for which the erythrasma served as portal of entry.
Fever, chills, and systemic illness alongside a skin fold infection — requires prompt medical evaluation to rule out deeper infection or sepsis.
Extensive erythrasma covering large body areas in an immunocompromised patient, warranting urgent assessment to exclude deeper bacterial involvement.

causes.description: This Gram-positive diphtheroid is a normal commensal on human skin; when local conditions shift — increased warmth, moisture, skin-fold occlusion, or reduced immune surveillance — it proliferates within the stratum corneum, producing a mixture of porphyrins and lipases that break down the keratin layer and produce the characteristic discolouration and fluorescence.

The intertrigo microclimate of body folds creates conditions of sustained warmth (above 30°C), high relative humidity, and reduced air circulation that dramatically accelerate C. minutissimum proliferation; this explains the predilection for the groin, axillae, toe webs, and inframammary skin.

Elevated blood glucose alters the composition of skin surface secretions, providing enriched nutrients for C. minutissimum and simultaneously impairing the innate immune response; the prevalence of erythrasma in diabetics is approximately 20-25%, compared with 4-8% in the general population.

Excess body weight creates deep skin folds where friction, moisture, and sweating produce a sustained intertrigo microclimate; the skin fold surface area and occlusion increase proportionally with BMI, explaining the strong association between obesity and intertriginous erythrasma.

Pathological or constitutionally heavy sweating maintains skin moisture in fold areas regardless of ambient temperature, providing a continuously favourable environment for C. minutissimum colonisation beyond the commensal threshold.

HIV infection, corticosteroid therapy, biological immunosuppressants, and organ transplantation reduce the cutaneous immune surveillance that normally keeps C. minutissimum at commensal levels; immunosuppressed individuals develop more extensive, treatment-resistant erythrasma more readily than immunocompetent adults.

riskFactors.description: Diabetics have approximately 2- to 3-fold higher prevalence of erythrasma; elevated blood glucose enriches skin surface secretions as a bacterial nutrient source and impairs innate immune defence, creating conditions where C. minutissimum grows unchecked.

Obesity creates and deepens intertriginous folds, increases sweating, and produces friction-related skin changes that provide an ideal microenvironment for erythrasma; a BMI above 35 is among the strongest independent predictors.

Ambient temperatures above 25°C combined with high humidity accelerate bacterial proliferation on skin; erythrasma prevalence of 20-25% in tropical regions compared with 4-8% in temperate climates reflects this environmental influence.

Excessive sweating, whether from heat, anxiety, or a primary sweating disorder, maintains the moist skin-fold microclimate that favours C. minutissimum overgrowth; treatment of hyperhidrosis can reduce erythrasma recurrence.

Any cause of impaired cellular immunity — HIV, transplant immunosuppression, high-dose corticosteroids, or biological agents — reduces the skin's ability to limit commensal bacteria; more extensive or refractory erythrasma is a recognised manifestation in these groups.

riskFactors.factor: Poor skin hygiene or infrequent bathing
riskFactors.description: Insufficient removal of skin surface bacteria allows gradual accumulation of C. minutissimum in fold areas; regular thorough washing with antiseptic or antibacterial soap is a cornerstone of prevention.

Skin fold depth, skin thinning, and reduced skin barrier function increase with age; older adults also have higher rates of diabetes, obesity, and relative immunosenescence, all of which compound erythrasma risk.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-60, particularly in warm climates or those with obesity or diabetes
People with hyperhidrosis (excessive sweating) regardless of body weight
Tropical-region populations, where 20-25% prevalence rates are documented
Immunocompromised individuals including those with HIV or on immunosuppressive therapy
Athletes and manual workers who spend prolonged periods in moist, warm skin-fold conditions
Elderly adults with skin-fold changes and comorbidities including diabetes and reduced mobility
diagnosisOverview: Erythrasma is primarily a clinical and Wood's lamp diagnosis. The first step is to examine the affected skin fold under a Wood's lamp (365 nm ultraviolet light) in a darkened room; active erythrasma fluoresces coral-red or bright pink, a distinctive finding that confirms the diagnosis immediately. This fluorescence can be absent if the patient has recently washed the affected area, as porphyrins are water-soluble and may be removed by bathing. In that case, re-examination after 48-72 hours without bathing restores the fluorescence. When Wood's lamp findings are equivocal or when tinea cannot be clinically excluded, a skin scraping is taken from the lesion margin, treated with potassium hydroxide (KOH), and examined under the microscope; the absence of fungal hyphae or spores on KOH preparation supports a bacterial cause. Gram stain of a scraping may show small Gram-positive rods consistent with Corynebacterium, though this is rarely required in routine practice. Bacterial culture on special media can confirm C. minutissimum but is rarely necessary for straightforward cases. In extensive or treatment-resistant erythrasma, blood glucose measurement or HbA1c testing is essential to exclude undiagnosed or poorly controlled diabetes. The key differential diagnoses are tinea cruris (ringworm of the groin), pityriasis versicolor, inverse psoriasis, and contact dermatitis — each distinguished by KOH examination, Wood's lamp findings, and clinical morphology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the coral-red fluorescence of C. minutissimum porphyrins, providing immediate, non-invasive confirmation of active erythrasma; the most specific and rapid diagnostic test available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in a darkened room; a handheld ultraviolet lamp is held 10-15 cm from the affected skin and the characteristic fluorescence is observed; takes under one minute.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes tinea cruris (dermatophyte infection) as a co-infection or mimic; absent hyphae or spores on KOH preparation support a bacterial diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blunt edge or curette lightly scrapes skin scales from the lesion margin onto a microscope slide; potassium hydroxide is added and the slide is examined under a microscope within minutes to hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates small Gram-positive rods consistent with Corynebacterium minutissimum when the diagnosis remains uncertain after Wood's lamp and KOH examination.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A scraping is placed on a slide, fixed, and stained; laboratory results take one to two hours; rarely needed in routine dermatology practice.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for undiagnosed or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus, the strongest modifiable risk factor for erythrasma, particularly in patients presenting with extensive or recurrent disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; fasting glucose and HbA1c results are available within hours and provide both diagnostic and prognostic information.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms C. minutissimum identity in atypical or extensive cases or when antimicrobial resistance is suspected; rarely necessary in practice.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab from the lesion is sent to the laboratory; C. minutissimum grows slowly and results may take up to seven days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of erythrasma depends on the extent of disease, the body site, and predisposing comorbidities. For localised intertriginous erythrasma affecting one or two skin fold areas, topical antibiotics are first-line and highly effective. Topical erythromycin gel or solution applied twice daily for seven days, or topical clindamycin applied twice daily for seven days, achieves cure in approximately 60-80% of limited cases. Fusidic acid cream is another topical option with good activity against C. minutissimum. For toe-web erythrasma, an antiseptic approach — regular applications of Whitfield's ointment (benzoic acid compound) or aluminium chloride solution — can also reduce bacterial burden while addressing the moisture component. For widespread or multifocal erythrasma, or when topical therapy has failed, oral erythromycin is the first-line systemic antibiotic; the standard regimen is 250-500 mg four times daily for 14 days and achieves cure rates of approximately 80-100%. Single-dose oral azithromycin 1 g is an increasingly used alternative for patients who struggle to adhere to a two-week course, showing cure rates of 70-90% in small trials. Tetracyclines and clarithromycin are alternatives when erythromycin is not tolerated. Addressing predisposing factors is critical to preventing recurrence: optimising glycaemic control in diabetics, reducing body weight in those with obesity, treating hyperhidrosis with aluminium chloride antiperspirant, and improving skin-fold hygiene. Co-existing tinea pedis, tinea cruris, or candidal intertrigo should be identified and treated simultaneously to prevent reinfection.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical erythromycin (2% gel or solution)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line topical antibiotic for limited erythrasma; applied to the affected skin twice daily for seven days; well tolerated and effective for single-site intertriginous disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinical and mycological cure in approximately 60-80% of limited erythrasma cases in published case series and small trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical clindamycin (1% solution or lotion)
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative first-line topical option; applied twice daily for seven days; particularly useful in patients with erythromycin intolerance or where macrolide resistance is a concern.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar efficacy to topical erythromycin; achieves cure in approximately 60-75% of localised erythrasma.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral erythromycin
medicalTreatments.description: First-line systemic antibiotic for extensive, multifocal, or topical-therapy-resistant erythrasma; given as 250-500 mg four times daily for 14 days; the standard regimen in guidelines.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves cure in approximately 80-100% of cases in published series; erythromycin remains active against C. minutissimum in most settings.

medicalTreatments.name: Single-dose oral azithromycin 1 g
medicalTreatments.description: A single-dose macrolide option that improves adherence for patients unable to complete a two-week course; increasing evidence supports its efficacy as a first-line systemic treatment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of approximately 70-90% in small randomised trials; comparable to standard erythromycin when adherence to the longer course is uncertain.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical fusidic acid cream
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative topical option with activity against C. minutissimum; applied two to three times daily for five to seven days; useful when erythromycin or clindamycin is not available.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical clearance rates of approximately 60-75% for localised disease in reported case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Aluminium chloride solution (20%)
medicalTreatments.description: Not a direct antibiotic but reduces hyperhidrosis that sustains the moist skin-fold environment; applied to affected areas once to twice daily as an adjunct or maintenance preventive strategy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces axillary and intertriginous sweating by 50-80% and lowers erythrasma recurrence rates when used as part of a maintenance hygiene regimen.

alternativeTreatments.name: Whitfield's ointment (benzoic acid compound)
alternativeTreatments.description: A topical antifungal and antibacterial preparation that has been used historically for toe-web erythrasma; reduces bacterial and fungal burden simultaneously; still used in low-resource settings where antibiotics are less accessible.

alternativeTreatments.name: Regular chlorhexidine antiseptic washes
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily washing of affected skin folds with 2-4% chlorhexidine gluconate solution reduces surface bacterial counts and is a useful adjunct to antibiotic treatment, particularly for patients with recurrent erythrasma.

whySeeSpecialist: Most erythrasma is managed well by primary care with Wood's lamp confirmation and topical antibiotics. A dermatologist should be consulted when the diagnosis is uncertain and tinea cannot be excluded, when disease is extensive or fails to respond to standard treatment, or when erythrasma recurs despite addressing predisposing factors. In patients with HIV or those on immunosuppressants, dermatology input ensures that rare severe presentations are managed appropriately and underlying immune status is considered in the treatment plan.
doctorSelectionGuide: A primary care physician or general dermatologist is appropriate for most cases. Look for access to Wood's lamp examination — ideally in clinic — and willingness to test for diabetes in recurrent cases. For patients in whom tinea coexists or cannot be excluded, a dermatologist experienced in infectious skin diseases can perform simultaneous KOH microscopy. Patients with severe obesity, poorly controlled diabetes, or immunosuppression benefit from coordinated management with an endocrinologist or the prescribing specialist to address the underlying predisposing condition.
preventionStrategies: Keep skin folds clean and dry: wash daily with antibacterial soap and dry fold areas thoroughly, particularly the groin, armpits, and toe webs.
Apply a talc-free absorbent powder or aluminium chloride-based antiperspirant to affected folds to reduce moisture accumulation.
Achieve and maintain a healthy weight, as obesity is the strongest modifiable risk factor for intertriginous erythrasma.
Optimise glycaemic control if you have diabetes — tight glucose control reduces erythrasma prevalence substantially.
Treat coexisting tinea pedis and tinea cruris promptly, as their presence creates a more favourable microenvironment for C. minutissimum.
Wear breathable, moisture-wicking clothing (cotton or technical fabric) to reduce skin-fold humidity.
lifestyleModifications: Dry skin folds thoroughly after bathing — a hair dryer on the cool setting is effective for the groin and inframammary areas.
Change underwear and socks daily and choose breathable natural fabrics over synthetic materials.
Lose weight through diet and exercise if obesity is contributing to persistent skin-fold maceration.
Avoid prolonged periods of wetness or sweating without the opportunity to wash and dry affected areas.
Complete the full antibiotic course; stopping early when symptoms improve is a common cause of recurrence.
Treat underlying conditions actively — particularly diabetes and hyperhidrosis — to address the root cause.

recommended: A low-glycaemic-index diet for diabetic patients to maintain blood glucose below levels that enrich skin surface secretions
Adequate dietary protein and micronutrients (zinc, vitamin A, vitamin C) to support skin barrier repair
Plenty of water to reduce concentrated eccrine sweat secretions that contribute to skin-fold moisture
High-fibre foods that support weight management and reduce the obesity-related skin fold maceration
avoid: Excess refined carbohydrates and sugar in diabetics or pre-diabetics, which elevate blood glucose and skin surface nutrient content
Crash diets, which deplete essential nutrients needed for skin barrier maintenance and immune function
Excessive alcohol, which impairs immunity and contributes to weight gain

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is encouraged and helps reduce obesity, one of the main risk factors for erythrasma. During and after exercise, prompt showering and thorough drying of skin folds are essential to prevent bacterial overgrowth. Moisture-wicking workout clothing reduces sweat accumulation. During treatment, affected areas should be covered if possible and exercise clothing laundered after each use. Swimming is acceptable once the skin is not actively fissured.
prognosis: The outlook for erythrasma is excellent in most patients. With appropriate topical or oral antibiotic therapy, the infection clears in one to four weeks and fluorescence under Wood's lamp disappears. Residual post-inflammatory brown discolouration may take several weeks to fade. The main long-term challenge is recurrence: erythrasma relapses in approximately 30-40% of patients within six months when predisposing conditions — obesity, diabetes, hyperhidrosis — are not addressed. Recurrence rates fall significantly with consistent skin-fold hygiene, antiperspirant use, and management of the underlying cause. Erythrasma does not progress to systemic infection in immunocompetent individuals, but the toe-web form warrants particular attention as a recognised risk factor for recurrent lower limb cellulitis — a potentially serious complication that is largely preventable with prompt erythrasma treatment.
recoveryTimeline: Topical antibiotic treatment reduces symptoms — itching and scaling — within three to five days, with visible lightening of the reddish-brown plaques by the end of a seven-day course. Wood's lamp fluorescence disappears in one to two weeks of treatment, confirming bacterial clearance. Oral erythromycin achieves similar resolution within 10-14 days. Residual pigmentation may persist for four to eight weeks as post-inflammatory change but is cosmetically temporary. Patients should be re-examined with Wood's lamp at two to four weeks after treatment to confirm clearance before discharge.
complications: Lower limb cellulitis — toe-web erythrasma disrupts skin integrity and provides a portal of entry for Streptococcus or Staphylococcus, making it one of the most common identifiable precipitants of lower limb cellulitis; approximately 70% of cellulitis episodes in adults have toe-web disease at the portal of entry.

Recurrent erythrasma — approximately 30-40% of treated patients relapse within six months when predisposing factors are unaddressed; serial courses of antibiotics without addressing root causes are ineffective long-term.
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation — more pronounced in skin phototypes IV-VI (darker skin tones), lasting four to eight weeks after successful treatment; usually resolves without intervention.

Coexistent tinea — erythrasma and tinea cruris or tinea pedis frequently coexist in the same skin fold areas; untreated tinea after clearing erythrasma is a common cause of apparent antibiotic failure.

Psychological impact — visible skin fold discolouration, chronic pruritus, and recurrence cause distress and avoidance of social situations such as swimming; acknowledging this and ensuring effective treatment improves quality of life.

supportResources.name: British Association of Dermatologists — patient information
supportResources.description: UK patient resources from the British Association of Dermatologists, covering a wide range of skin conditions including bacterial skin infections.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Dermatology — Skin Infections
supportResources.description: Patient education resources from the AAD on identifying and treating various skin infections, including erythrasma and related conditions.

supportResources.name: DermNet NZ — Erythrasma
supportResources.description: Comprehensive, doctor-reviewed patient information on erythrasma including clinical features, diagnosis, and treatment from a leading dermatology reference site.

sources.title: Greywal T et al. Evidence-based recommendations for the management of erythrasma. Journal of the American Academy of Dermatology, 2016
Sobera JO, Elewski BE. Fungal diseases. In: Dermatology, Elsevier, 2003 (erythrasma chapter)
Avci O et al. Erythrasma: Forgotten but not rare. Pediatric Dermatology, 2013
Haas N et al. Single-dose azithromycin for the treatment of erythrasma. Acta Dermato-Venereologica, 2004
Gunduz K et al. Diabetes mellitus and erythrasma: a link. Journal of Dermatology, 2004

confusedWithConditions.name: Pityriasis versicolor
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pityriasis versicolor is caused by Malassezia fungus and produces hypo- or hyperpigmented patches across the trunk and neck with yellow-green fluorescence under Wood's lamp; erythrasma is bacterial, located in body folds, and fluoresces coral-red.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic dermatitis (eczema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema produces intensely itchy, lichenified, weeping plaques without the characteristic flat brownish scale of erythrasma; Wood's lamp shows no coral-red fluorescence in eczema, and KOH and bacterial culture are negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Inverse psoriasis affects skin folds with smooth, bright red, well-demarcated plaques without scale (in contrast to classical plaque psoriasis) and may closely mimic erythrasma; Wood's lamp fluorescence is absent, and nail, scalp, or joint involvement suggests psoriasis.

relatedConditions.name: Pyoderma
relatedConditions.relevance: Pyoderma and erythrasma are both bacterial skin infections; they may coexist in the same patient, and toe-web erythrasma is a recognised portal of entry for cellulitis, one of the deeper pyodermas.

relatedConditions.name: Pityriasis versicolor
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important differential diagnosis; both cause flat, scaly, pigmented patches on the skin, but pityriasis versicolor is fungal and fluoresces a different colour under Wood's lamp.

relatedConditions.name: Impetigo
relatedConditions.relevance: A superficial bacterial skin infection that shares the same body-surface location as erythrasma; distinguished by its honey-coloured crusting and vesicle formation versus the flat, scaling patches of erythrasma.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inverse psoriasis affecting skin folds closely mimics erythrasma clinically; careful Wood's lamp examination and skin scraping avoid misdiagnosis and inappropriate antibiotic use.

faqs.question: What is erythrasma?
faqs.answer: Erythrasma is a superficial bacterial skin infection caused by Corynebacterium minutissimum. It produces flat, reddish-brown, mildly scaly patches in warm, moist skin folds such as the groin, armpits, and between the toes. It is not contagious under normal circumstances and responds well to antibiotic treatment.

faqs.question: What does erythrasma look like?
faqs.answer: Erythrasma appears as flat, well-defined, reddish-brown or tan patches with fine surface scale in skin folds. There are no blisters or pustules. Under an ultraviolet Wood's lamp in a dark room, the patches glow a distinctive coral-red or pink colour, which is the hallmark diagnostic sign.

faqs.question: Is erythrasma the same as ringworm?
faqs.answer: No. Ringworm (tinea cruris) is a fungal infection that causes ring-shaped, itchy plaques with a scaly, raised border in the groin. Erythrasma is a bacterial infection producing flat, brownish patches without a ring shape. A skin scraping test (KOH) shows fungi in ringworm and is negative in erythrasma.

faqs.question: How is erythrasma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Erythrasma is diagnosed by examining affected skin with a Wood's lamp — a handheld ultraviolet light — in a dark room. Active erythrasma glows coral-red due to porphyrins produced by the bacteria. A skin scraping examined under a microscope after KOH treatment rules out fungal infection. Blood glucose is checked in recurrent cases.

faqs.question: What antibiotic treats erythrasma?
faqs.answer: Topical erythromycin gel or clindamycin solution applied twice daily for seven days clears limited erythrasma. Extensive or resistant cases are treated with oral erythromycin 250-500 mg four times daily for 14 days, or a single 1 g dose of azithromycin. Fusidic acid cream is an alternative topical option.

faqs.question: How long does erythrasma take to clear with treatment?
faqs.answer: Symptoms such as itching and scaling usually improve within three to five days of starting topical antibiotics. The brownish patches visibly lighten over one to two weeks. Wood's lamp fluorescence disappears within two weeks, confirming bacterial clearance. Some residual skin discolouration may take another four to eight weeks to fade fully.

faqs.question: Does erythrasma keep coming back?
faqs.answer: Yes, it recurs in approximately 30-40% of patients within six months if the predisposing conditions are not addressed. The most common reasons are persistent obesity, poorly controlled diabetes, ongoing hyperhidrosis, or inadequate skin-fold hygiene. Treating the underlying cause substantially reduces recurrence.

faqs.question: Is erythrasma contagious?
faqs.answer: Erythrasma is not contagious in the usual sense — it is caused by a bacterium that normally lives on skin and overgrows when local conditions favour it. You cannot catch erythrasma from another person through casual contact. The bacteria require the right combination of warmth, moisture, and skin condition to cause infection.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause erythrasma?
faqs.answer: Yes. People with diabetes have approximately 2- to 3-fold higher rates of erythrasma. Elevated blood glucose enriches the nutrients available on the skin surface for bacteria, and impaired immune defence allows C. minutissimum to grow beyond its normal commensal level. Improving glucose control reduces the risk of recurrence.

faqs.question: Can erythrasma cause cellulitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Toe-web erythrasma causes maceration and fissuring between the toes that breaks the skin barrier and allows Streptococcus or Staphylococcus to enter deeper tissue. This makes toe-web erythrasma one of the most common identifiable causes of lower limb cellulitis. Treating erythrasma promptly reduces the risk of this complication.

faqs.question: What is the coral-red fluorescence of erythrasma?
faqs.answer: The coral-red glow seen under a Wood's lamp is produced by porphyrins — pigmented compounds that Corynebacterium minutissimum generates as part of its metabolism within the stratum corneum. These porphyrins absorb ultraviolet light and emit visible red-pink light. The fluorescence disappears after successful treatment as the bacteria are eliminated.

faqs.question: What is the difference between erythrasma and pityriasis versicolor?
faqs.answer: Both cause flat, scaly, discoloured patches on the skin. Erythrasma is bacterial, occurs in body folds, and fluoresces coral-red under Wood's lamp. Pityriasis versicolor is caused by Malassezia fungus, affects the trunk and neck, shows yellow-green Wood's lamp fluorescence, and has positive KOH microscopy with fungal elements.

faqs.question: How do I stop erythrasma from coming back?
faqs.answer: Prevent recurrence by keeping skin folds clean and dry, using an aluminium chloride antiperspirant on affected areas, losing weight if obesity is contributing, controlling blood glucose if you have diabetes, wearing breathable clothing, and treating any coexisting fungal infections of the feet or groin promptly.

faqs.question: Can erythrasma affect children?
faqs.answer: Erythrasma primarily affects adults, with peak prevalence in those aged 20-60. It is uncommon in prepubertal children because the hormonal and sweat gland changes that favour C. minutissimum overgrowth are less pronounced. When erythrasma does occur in a child, it should prompt evaluation for diabetes or another predisposing condition.

faqs.question: Does erythrasma smell?
faqs.answer: Erythrasma itself does not cause an odour directly, but the warm, moist skin-fold environment that promotes it can contribute to general skin odour from bacterial breakdown of sweat. The porphyrins produced are not odorous. Effective treatment combined with improved skin-fold hygiene and use of antiperspirant usually resolves any associated odour.

faqs.question: Is erythrasma serious?
faqs.answer: In immunocompetent people, erythrasma is not serious — it is a superficial, benign skin infection that responds readily to antibiotics. The main concerns are recurrence and, specifically for toe-web erythrasma, the risk of triggering lower limb cellulitis. In people with HIV or severe immunosuppression, more extensive disease can occasionally develop.

faqs.question: Can erythrasma affect the armpits?
faqs.answer: Yes. The axillae (armpits) are one of the three most common sites for erythrasma alongside the groin and toe webs. Axillary erythrasma causes brownish, slightly scaly patches in the armpit fold and is often associated with hyperhidrosis. It may be mistaken for deodorant residue or skin discolouration and can persist for years if not treated.

faqs.question: Should I stop using antifungal cream if I have erythrasma instead of ringworm?
faqs.answer: Yes. Erythrasma does not respond to antifungal creams because it is a bacterial, not fungal, infection. If your skin fold rash has not improved after two to three weeks of antifungal treatment, erythrasma should be considered. A Wood's lamp examination by a doctor can confirm this within minutes. Antibiotic treatment will then clear it.

faqs.question: Does Wood's lamp examination hurt?
faqs.answer: No. A Wood's lamp examination is entirely painless and non-invasive. A handheld ultraviolet lamp is held a short distance from the affected skin in a darkened room. The examination takes under a minute. The only preparation needed is to avoid washing the affected area for 24-48 hours before the appointment, as water removes the porphyrins that produce the fluorescence.

faqs.question: Is erythrasma related to tinea versicolor?
faqs.answer: They are different infections that are sometimes confused because both cause flat, discoloured, scaly patches on the skin. Tinea versicolor (pityriasis versicolor) is caused by a yeast called Malassezia and affects the trunk and neck. Erythrasma is a bacterial infection in body folds. Both are diagnosed by Wood's lamp and KOH skin scraping, which give clearly different results for each.

regionalNames.name: Chamdi ki bacterial rang mein badlav

metaDescription: Erythrasma is a bacterial skin fold infection causing brown scaly patches, diagnosed by coral-red Wood's lamp glow. Learn causes, treatment, and prevention.
